sma.bidari-ombattu.about: An interpretive Bidri (Bidriware) setting: nine men's morris played on a blackened-brass board inlaid with project-original silver tarkashi and floral work.
sma.bidari-ombattu.attribution.board: Project-original Bidri-style blackened-brass and silver board artwork.
sma.bidari-ombattu.attribution.table: Project-original Bidri-style seamless silver-diaper tabletop tile.
sma.bidari-ombattu.claim.bidri: Bidri (Bidriware) is a documented metal-inlay craft of Bidar, Karnataka — blackened white-brass with pure-silver inlay — geographical-indication registered in 2006.
sma.bidari-ombattu.claim.interpretive: The black-metal-and-silver look is an artistic homage; this board is not an authentic Bidriware object and no historical claim is made that the game used Bidri boards.
sma.bidari-ombattu.disclosure.interpreti: Bidari Ombattu is a digital homage in the Bidri idiom. It uses no protected emblem, no copied artisan motif, and implies no real silver or endangered material.
sma.bidari-ombattu.intent: Line up three on the silver grid; every mill you complete lets you lift a rival piece — see the row before you place.
sma.bidari-ombattu.teaching.lose-1: Reduced to two, you can no longer form a mill. The steadier hand took the board.
sma.bidari-ombattu.teaching.mill-1: Three in a row — a mill, bright as inlaid silver. What you align, you may break.
sma.bidari-ombattu.teaching.remove-1: Lift a rival piece — never one already inside a mill unless every rival piece is. Take the piece that guards a junction.
sma.bidari-ombattu.teaching.start-1: Nine pieces each on a blackened-silver board. Complete a connected line of three — a mill — to break a rival piece. Plan the line before you place.
sma.bidari-ombattu.teaching.win-1: The rival is down to two and can form no line. Patience, like the slow inlay of silver, has won the board.
sma.bidari-ombattu.title: Bidari Ombattu — Nine on Blackened Silver
